candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture be made discriminating rather than merely interpretive: what observable institutional patterns would distinguish a system where redistribution is structurally complementary to inherited advantage from one where redistribution genuinely targets reproduction mechanisms but is just too weak or politically underpowered to succeed?

Which upstream stocks are actually most load-bearing in intergenerational inequality reproduction, and how do they convert into one another over the life course? The conjecture lists wealth, schooling, health, expectations, networks, trust, and family time, but it remains open which are primary drivers, which are downstream proxies, and where intervention would have the highest leverage.

What institutional designs could rewrite the inheritance architecture of advantage without generating compensating adaptations, new positional races, or unacceptable losses in family autonomy and pluralism? This is unresolved because families and elites may convert advantages into new scarce goods whenever old channels are equalized.
